name: Line-of-Sight Detection
description: Line-of-Sight Detection is a non-player behavior mechanic that controls sensing, intention, movement, and response.
design_notes: Line-of-Sight Detection should be designed around a clear player promise, a readable trigger, and a predictable result. For ai mechanics, the important design work is deciding when the mechanic is allowed, how much commitment it creates, what feedback confirms success, and what counter-pressure keeps it from solving every situation. Strong tuning makes the mechanic feel intentional even when it is used repeatedly or combined with adjacent systems.
design_purpose: Give the player a readable way to use line-of-sight detection as part of the core loop.
Create meaningful choices around timing, cost, risk, positioning, or long-term planning.
Connect line-of-sight detection to adjacent systems so it supports the larger game design instead of acting as an isolated feature.
player_fantasy: The player feels the world responds with believable intent instead of static scripting.

parameters.name: sense_radius
parameters.description: Area within which the AI can consider stimuli.

parameters.name: decision_interval
parameters.description: How often the AI re-evaluates the behavior.

parameters.name: priority_weight
parameters.description: Relative importance compared with competing AI goals.

edge_cases: Line-of-Sight Detection is triggered during a scene transition, pause, respawn, or save/load boundary.
Line-of-Sight Detection overlaps with another high-priority state such as damage, stun, dialogue, menu focus, or cutscene control.
Line-of-Sight Detection runs at extreme frame rates, high latency, very large coordinates, or after long play sessions.
common_bugs: Line-of-Sight Detection state does not reset correctly after interruption or reload.
Feedback for Line-of-Sight Detection plays even when the mechanic fails or is rejected.
Line-of-Sight Detection produces different results between frame rates, platforms, or network clients.
balancing_notes: Make Line-of-Sight Detection strong enough to justify attention but limited enough that other mechanics keep their purpose.
Tune costs, cooldowns, ranges, and feedback together; changing only one number can shift the mechanic from optional to dominant.
accessibility_notes: Provide clear visual, audio, or haptic feedback when Line-of-Sight Detection becomes available, succeeds, or fails.
Avoid relying on a single sensory cue or extremely narrow timing unless the game offers assists or alternate settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Implement Line-of-Sight Detection with engine-agnostic state first, then bind it to MonoBehaviour, CharacterController, Rigidbody, UI Toolkit, or Netcode systems as appropriate.
implementation_notes.godot: Model Line-of-Sight Detection as a node or resource-driven state and update deterministic pieces from _physics_process when timing or collision matters.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement Line-of-Sight Detection with Actor Components, CharacterMovement, Gameplay Ability System, UMG, or replicated gameplay state depending on the mechanic scope.
implementation_notes.web: Implement Line-of-Sight Detection as data-owned state updated from the main loop; keep rendering, input, and simulation boundaries explicit for Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or custom JavaScript.

example_games.title: Metal Gear Solid
example_games.note: Uses sight cones to support stealth play.

example_games.title: Mark of the Ninja
example_games.note: Shows detection information to the player.
